text: This instructional video depicts the surgical management of benign prostatic hyperplasia with robotic-assisted laparoscopic simple supraphibic prostatectomy. Our patient is a seventy-year-old male with a long history of lower urinary tract symptoms, in addition to urinary retention and bladder stones. Physical exam revealed a very large non-tender prostate without nodules. His post-void residual is a hundred cc's. Creatinine is one point O and PSA eighteen point five with a high serum four case score. Marked here is the PI-RADS III lesion in the right medial peripheral zone on MRI fusion biopsy. Prostate size was estimated at two hundred seven grams. Biopsy revealed no evidence of malignancy. CAT scan shows extent of his bladder stones and prostatic enlargement.
Several weeks after his biopsy, he was seen in the emergency room for bilateral flank pain. CAT scan showed bilateral hydronephrosis. He was taken to the operating room for ureteral stents.

After discussing various treatment options, the patient decided to undergo a robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy with removal of his bladder stones. Optimal candidates for simple prostatectomy is generally reserved for prostates larger than one hundred grams. The suprapubic approach is well suited for patients of concurrent bladder stones or diverticula. Minimally invasive methods utilizing robotic assisted surgery has become commonplace.

The surgical team begins the procedure by placing ports, positioning the patient in a modified dorsal lithotomy steep Trendelenburg position. The robotic system is positioned between the patient's legs. Maryland bipolar forceps are placed in the left robotic arm, monopolar scissors in the right arm, and tenaculum forceps in the third arm. The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the outline of the bladder ahead.

The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the left medial umbilical ligament, followed by a similar incision on the right side. After completing both incisions, the medial umbilical ligaments and the urachus are transected. The space of Retzius is developed using a combination of blunt dissection and cautery.

The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the pubic bone, with the bladder and prostate visible in the field. The developed space of Retzius is evident, and the Foley catheter is manipulated to help identify the bladder neck.

The surgeon opens the bladder transversely approximately three centimeters above the bladder neck using monopolar cautery. The Foley catheter balloon becomes visible, and the tenaculum forceps are used to retract the anterior lip of the bladder wall. The bladder wall is opened further until reaching the lateral limits of the prostate. Bladder stones are encountered and removed using a specimen retrieval bag through the eleven millimeter assistant port.

The surgeon removes five bladder stones using a specimen retrieval bag through the assistant port. The ureteral stents, placed preoperatively due to prostate-induced ureteral obstruction, are visible.

The surgeon uses tenaculum forceps to identify the posterior bladder neck and begins dissection between the prostate adenoma and prostatic capsule. Bipolar and monopolar cautery are used to control bleeding while extending the dissection plane circumferentially. The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the capsule with the aid of blunt dissection and retraction.

The surgeon moves the dissection anteriorly after reaching the posterior and lateral limits. A zero bicoral suture on a CT needle is placed on the anterior bladder as a holding stitch, which is brought through the anterior abdominal wall using a fascial closure device. The dissection continues anteriorly around the prostate, with the adenoma nearly free from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the completely freed prostate adenoma into a specimen retrieval bag for removal, dividing the specimen into two pieces for extraction.

The surgeon uses microsutures to control the pedicles at the five and seven o'clock positions. A 2-0 microsuture is used to approximate the urethra to the trigone of the bladder with a surgical slip knot. Five interrupted 2-0 microsutures are placed in total, and the urethral stents are removed.

The surgeon inserts a 22 French three-way Foley catheter into the bladder. The bladder closure is performed in two layers using running 2-0 Vicryl sutures. The bladder is filled with 200 cc of normal saline to check for leaks, and no leakage is observed.

Under laparoscopic visualization, the surgeon places a closed suction device. All trocars are removed, and the assistant port is closed if necessary. The specimen is extracted, and the skin and fascia are sutured closed.

Robotic time was a hundred and sixty-four minutes, total operative time one hundred and ninety-eight minutes, and estimated blood loss eight hundred and fifty milliliters. Continuous bladder irrigation is ran overnight and clamped on post-op day one. He was kept on clear liquids until passage of flatus. Close suction drain was removed after fluid creatinine was consistent with serum creatinine. He was discharged on post-op day number two.

The patient is seen postoperatively in one week and was without complaints. Cystogram showed no leak and the catheter was removed. He will follow up in three months with a post-void residual, blood work, and renal ultrasound. Final pathology showed BPH tissue without evidence of malignancy. We have performed twenty-one robotic simple prostatectomies. Median prostate size is a hundred and seventy-seven grams. Median robotic time is a hundred and sixty-five minutes with an estimated blood loss of four hundred and fifty cc's. Median stay in the hospital is one point five days. There have been a total of four complications all Clavian II. All patients are emptying adequately without incontinence and we have not had any bladder neck contractures. Robotic simple prostatectomy provides good patient outcomes with low morbidity.
This concludes our instructional video on robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy.
